thi13:1.0.252: My hair was as black as bees,graced with curly tips;now old, it has become like hemp bark—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.253: Crowned with flowers,my head was as fragrant as a perfume box;now old, it smells like dog fur—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.254: My hair was as thick as a well-planted forest,it shone, parted with brush and pins;now old, it’s patchy and sparse—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.255: With plaits of black and ribbons of gold,it was so pretty, adorned with braids;now old, my head’s gone bald—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.256: My eyebrows used to look so nice,like crescents painted by an artist;now old, they droop with wrinkles—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.257: My eyes shone brilliant as gems,wide and deepest blue;ruined by age, they shine no more—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.258: My nose was like a perfect peak,lovely in my bloom of youth;now old, it’s shriveled like a pepper;the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.259: My ear-lobes were so pretty,like lovingly crafted bracelets;now old, they droop with wrinkles—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.260: My teeth used to be so pretty,bright as a jasmine flower;now old, they’re broken and yellow—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.261: My singing was sweet as a cuckoowandering in the forest groves;now old, it’s patchy and croaking—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.262: My neck used to be so pretty,like a polished shell of conch;now old, it’s bowed and bent—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.263: My arms used to be so pretty,like rounded cross-bars;now old, they droop like a trumpet-flower tree—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.264: My hands used to be so pretty,adorned with lovely golden rings;now old, they’re like red radishes—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.265: My breasts used to be so pretty,swelling, round, close, and high;now they droop like water bags—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.266: My body used to be so pretty,like a polished slab of gold;now it’s covered with fine wrinkles—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.267: Both my thighs used to be so pretty,like an elephant’s trunk;now old, they’re like bamboo—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.268: My calves used to be so pretty,adorned with cute golden anklets;now old, they’re like sesame sticks—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.269: Both my feet used to be so pretty,plump as if with cotton-wool;now old, they’re cracked and wrinkly—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.270: This bag of bones once was such,but now it’s withered, home to so much pain;like a house in decay with plaster crumbling—the word of the truthful one is confirmed.
thi13:1.0.271: “You fell asleep saying ‘ascetics’;you woke up saying ‘ascetics’;you only praise ascetics, madam—surely you’ll become an ascetic.
thi13:1.0.272: You provide asceticswith abundant food and drink.I ask you now, Rohiṇī:why do you like ascetics?
thi13:1.0.273: They don’t like to work, they’re lazy,they live on charity;always on the lookout, greedy for sweets—so why do you like ascetics?”
thi13:1.0.274: “Dad, for a long time nowyou’ve questioned me about ascetics.I shall extol for youtheir wisdom, ethics, and vigor.
thi13:1.0.275: They like to work, they’re not lazy;by giving up greed and hate,they do the best kind of work—that’s why I like ascetics.
thi13:1.0.276: As for the three roots of evil,by pure deeds they shake them off.They have given up all wickedness—that’s why I like ascetics.
thi13:1.0.277: Their bodily actions are pure;their actions of speech likewise;their actions of mind are pure—that’s why I like ascetics.
thi13:1.0.278: Immaculate as a conch-shell,they’re pure inside and out,full of bright qualities—that’s why I like ascetics.
thi13:1.0.279: They’re learned and memorize the teaching,noble, living righteously,teaching the text and its meaning:that’s why I like ascetics.
thi13:1.0.280: They’re learned and memorize the teaching,noble, living righteously,unified in mind, and mindful—that’s why I like ascetics.
thi13:1.0.281: Traveling afar, and mindful,thoughtful in counsel, and stable,they understand the end of suffering—that’s why I like ascetics.
thi13:1.0.282: When they leave a village,they don’t look back with longing,but proceed without concern—that’s why I like ascetics.
thi13:1.0.283: They hoard no goods in storerooms,nor in pots or baskets.They seek food prepared by others—that’s why I like ascetics.
thi13:1.0.284: They don’t receive silver,or gold whether coined or uncoined;feeding on whatever comes that day,that’s why I like ascetics.
thi13:1.0.285: They have gone forth from different families,even different countries,and yet they all love one another—that’s why I like ascetics.”
thi13:1.0.286: “Dear Rohinī, it was truly for our benefitthat you were born in our family!You have faith and such keen respectfor the Buddha, his teaching, and the Saṅgha.
thi13:1.0.287: For you understand thissupreme field of merit.These ascetics will henceforthreceive our religious donation, too.
thi13:1.0.288: For there we will place our sacrifice,and it shall be abundant.”“If you fear suffering,if you don’t like suffering,
thi13:1.0.289: go for refuge to the Buddha, the poised,to his teaching and to the Saṅgha.Undertake the precepts,that will be good for you.”
thi13:1.0.290: “I go for refuge to the Buddha, the poised,to his teaching and to the Saṅgha.I undertake the precepts,that will be good for me.
thi13:1.0.291: In the past I was related to Brahmā,now I genuinely am a brahmin.Possessing the three knowledges, I’m a genuine scholar,I’m a knowledge master, a bathed initiate.”
thi13:1.0.292: “Once I carried a hermit’s staff,but these days I hunt deer.My desires have made me unable to crossfrom the awful marsh to the far shore.
thi13:1.0.293: Thinking me so in love with her,Cāpā kept our son happy.Having cut Cāpā’s bond,I’ll go forth once again.”
thi13:1.0.294: “Don’t be mad at me, great hero!Don’t be mad at me, great sage!If you’re mired in anger you can’t stay pure,let alone practice austerities.”
thi13:1.0.295: “I’m going to leave Nālā!For who’d stay here at Nālā!With their figures, the women trapascetics who live righteously.”
thi13:1.0.296: “Please, Kāḷa, come back to me.Enjoy pleasures like you did before.I’ll be under your control,along with any relatives I have.”
thi13:1.0.297: “Cāpā, if even a quarterof what you say were true,it would be a splendid thingfor a man in love with you!”
thi13:1.0.298: “Kāḷa, I am like a sprouting irisflowering on a mountain top,like a blossoming pomegranate,like a trumpet-flower tree on an isle;
thi13:1.0.299: my limbs are anointed with yellow sandalwood,and I wear the finest Kāsi cloth:when I am so very beautiful,how can you abandon me and leave?”
thi13:1.0.300: “You’re like a fowlerwho wants to catch a bird;but you won’t trap mewith your captivating form.”
thi13:1.0.301: “But this child, my fruit,was begotten by you, Kāḷa.When I have this child,how can you abandon me and leave?”
thi13:1.0.302: “The wise give upchildren, family, and wealth.Great heroes go forthlike elephants breaking their bonds.”
thi13:1.0.303: “Now, this son of yours:I’ll strike him to the ground right here,with a stick or with a knife!Grieving your son, you will not leave.”
thi13:1.0.304: “Even if you feed our sonto jackals and dogs,I’d never return again, you bitch,not even for the child’s sake.”
thi13:1.0.305: “Well then, sir, tell me,where will you go, Kāḷa?To what village or town,city or capital?”
thi13:1.0.306: “Last time we had followers,we weren’t ascetics, we just thought we were.We wandered from village to village,to cities and capitals.
thi13:1.0.307: But now the Blessed One, the Buddha,on the bank of the Nerañjara River,teaches the Dhamma so that living creaturesmay abandon all suffering.I shall go to his presence,he shall be my Teacher.”
thi13:1.0.308: “Now please convey my respectsto the supreme protector of the world.Circling him to your right,dedicate my religious donation.”
thi13:1.0.309: “This is the proper thing to do,just as you have said to me.I’ll convey your respectsto the supreme protector of the world.Circling him to my right,I’ll dedicate your religious donation.”
thi13:1.0.310: Then Kāḷa set outfor the bank of the Nerañjara River.He saw the Awakened Oneteaching the deathless state:
thi13:1.0.311: suffering, suffering’s origin,suffering’s transcendence,and the noble eightfold paththat leads to the stilling of suffering.
thi13:1.0.312: He paid homage at his feet,circling him to his right,and conveyed Cāpā’s dedication;then he went forth to homelessness.He attained the three knowledges,and fulfilled the Buddha’s instructions.
thi13:1.0.313: “Before, when your children passed away,you would expose them to be eaten.All day and all nightyou’d be racked with despair.
thi13:1.0.314: Today, brahmin lady, you have exposedseven children in all to be eaten;Vāseṭṭhī, what is the reason whyyou’re not so filled with despair?”
thi13:1.0.315: “Many hundreds of children,hundreds of family circles,both mine and yours, brahmin,have been eaten in the past.
thi13:1.0.316: Having known the escapefrom rebirth and deathI neither grieve nor lament,nor do I despair.”
thi13:1.0.317: “Wow, Vaseṭṭhī, the words you speakreally are amazing!Whose teaching did you understandthat you say these things?”
thi13:1.0.318: “Brahmin, the Awakened Oneat the city of Mithilā,teaches the Dhamma so that living creaturesmay abandon all suffering.
thi13:1.0.319: After hearing the perfected one’s teaching,brahmin, which is free of all attachments,having understood the true teaching there,I’ve swept away grief for children.”
thi13:1.0.320: “I too shall goto the city of Mithilā.Hopefully the Buddha may release mefrom all suffering.”
thi13:1.0.321: The brahmin saw the Buddha,liberated, free of attachments.He taught him the Dhamma,the sage gone beyond suffering:
thi13:1.0.322: suffering, suffering’s origin,suffering’s transcendence,and the noble eightfold paththat leads to the stilling of suffering.
thi13:1.0.323: Having understood the true teaching there,he chose to go forth.Three days laterSujāta realized the three knowledges.
thi13:1.0.324: “Please, charioteer, go;take back this carriage.Bidding my brahmin lady good health, say:‘The brahmin has now gone forth.After three days,Sujāta realized the three knowledges.’”
thi13:1.0.325: Then taking the carriage,along with a thousand coins, the charioteerbade the brahmin lady good health, and said:“The brahmin has now gone forth.After three days,Sujāta realized the three knowledges.”
thi13:1.0.326: Hearing that the brahmin had the three knowledges, the lady replied:“I present to you this horse and carriage,O charioteer, along with 1000 coins,and a full bowl as a gift.”
thi13:1.0.327: “Keep the horse and carriage, lady,along with the thousand coins.I too shall go forth in his presence,the one of such splendid wisdom.”
thi13:1.0.328: “Elephants, cattle, jeweled earrings,such opulent domestic wealth:having given it up, your father went forth,enjoy these riches Sundarī,you are the family heir.”
thi13:1.0.329: “Elephants, cattle, jeweled earrings,such delightful domestic wealth:having given it up, my father went forth,racked by grief for his son.I too shall go forth,racked by grief for my brother.”
thi13:1.0.330: “Sundarī, may the wish you desirecome true.Leftovers as gleanings,and cast-off rags as robes—make do with these,free of defilements regarding the next life.”
thi13:1.0.331: “Ma’am, while I am still a trainee nun,my clairvoyance is clarified;I know my past lives,the places I used to live.
thi13:1.0.332: Relying on a fine lady like you,a senior nun who beautifies the Saṅgha,I’ve attained the three knowledges,and fulfilled the Buddha’s instructions.
thi13:1.0.333: Give me permission ma’am,I wish to go to Sāvatthī,where I shall roar my lion’s roarbefore the best of Buddhas.”
thi13:1.0.334: “Sundarī, see the Teacher!Golden colored, golden skinned,tamer of the untamed,the Awakened One who fears nothing from any quarter.”
thi13:1.0.335: “See Sundarī coming,liberated, free of attachments.desireless, detached,her task completed, without defilements.”
thi13:1.0.336: “Having set forth from Bārāṇasīand come to your presence, great hero,your disciple Sundarībows at your feet.
thi13:1.0.337: You are the Buddha, you are the Teacher,I am your rightful daughter, brahmin,born of your mouth.I’ve completed the task and am free of defilements.”
thi13:1.0.338: “Then welcome, good lady,you’re by no means unwelcome.For this is how the tamed comebowing at the Teacher’s feet;desireless, detached,the task completed, without defilements.”
thi13:1.0.339: “I was so young, my clothes so fresh,at that time I heard the teaching.Being diligent,I comprehended the truth;
thi13:1.0.340: and then I became profoundly dispassionatetowards all sensual pleasures.Seeing fear in identity,I longed for renunciation.
thi13:1.0.341: Giving up my family circle,bonded servants and workers,and my flourishing villages and lands,so delightful and pleasant,
thi13:1.0.342: I went forth;all that is no small wealth.Now that I’ve gone forth in faith like this,in the true teaching so well proclaimed,
thi13:1.0.343: since I desire to have nothing,it would not be appropriateto take back gold and money,having already got rid of them.
thi13:1.0.344: Money or golddoesn’t lead to peace and awakening.It doesn’t befit an ascetic,it’s not the wealth of the noble ones;
thi13:1.0.345: it’s just greed and vanity,confusion and growing decadence,dubious, troublesome—there is nothing lasting there.
thi13:1.0.346: Depraved and heedless,unenlightened folk, their hearts corrupt,fight each other,creating conflict.
thi13:1.0.347: Killing, caging, misery,loss, grief, and lamentation;those sunk in sensual pleasuressee many disastrous things.
thi13:1.0.348: My family, why do you urge me onto pleasures, as if you were my enemies?You know I’ve gone forth,seeing fear in sensual pleasures.
thi13:1.0.349: It’s not due to gold, coined or uncoined,that defilements come to an end.Sensual pleasures are enemies and murderers,hostile forces that bind you to thorns.
thi13:1.0.350: My family, why do you urge me onto pleasures, as if you were my enemies?You know I’ve gone forth,shaven, wrapped in my outer robe.
thi13:1.0.351: Leftovers as gleanings,and cast-off rags as robes—that’s what’s fitting for me,the essentials of the homeless life.
thi13:1.0.352: Great hermits expel sensual pleasures,both human and divine.Safe in their sanctuary, they are freed,having found unshakable happiness.
thi13:1.0.353: May I not encounter sensual pleasures,for no shelter is found in them.Sensual pleasures are enemies and murderers,as painful as a bonfire.
thi13:1.0.354: Greed is an obstacle, a threat,full of anguish and thorns;it is out of balance,a great gateway to confusion.
thi13:1.0.355: Hazardous and terrifying,sensual pleasures are like a snake’s head,where fools delight,the blind ordinary folk.
thi13:1.0.356: Stuck in the swamp of sensuality,there are so many ignorant in the world.They know nothing of the endof rebirth and death.
thi13:1.0.357: Because of sensual pleasures,people jump right on to the path that goes to a bad place.So many walk the paththat brings disease onto themselves.
thi13:1.0.358: That’s how sensual pleasures create enemies;they are so tormenting, so corrupting,trapping beings with the world’s material delights,they are nothing less than the bonds of death.
thi13:1.0.359: Maddening, enticing,sensual pleasures derange the mind.They’re a snare laid by Mārafor the corruption of beings.
thi13:1.0.360: Sensual pleasures are infinitely dangerous,they’re full of suffering, a terrible poison;offering little gratification, they’re makers of strife,withering bright qualities away.
thi13:1.0.361: Since I’ve created so much ruinationbecause of sensual pleasures,I will not relapse to them again,but will always delight in quenching.
thi13:1.0.362: Fighting against sensual pleasures,longing for that cool state,I shall meditate diligentlyfor the ending of all fetters.
thi13:1.0.363: Sorrowless, stainless, secure:I’ll follow that path,the straight noble eightfold wayby which the hermits have crossed over.”
thi13:1.0.364: It’s just eight days since she went forth,full of faith in the beautiful teaching.Guided by Uppalavaṇṇā,she is master of the three knowledges, conqueror of death.
thi13:1.0.365: This one is freed from slavery and debt,a nun with faculties developed.Detached from all attachments,she has completed the task and is free of defilements.”
thi13:1.0.367: Thus did Sakka, lord of all creatures,along with a host of gods,having come by their psychic powers,honor Subhā, the smith’s daughter.
